reasoning: Concise, patient-friendly summary of pulmonary hypertension causes and management.
output:
Pulmonary hypertension is high pressure in the lung arteries and can have many causes.

- Mechanism: Small lung arteries narrow or stiff, raising resistance and forcing the right side of the heart to work harder.
- Common causes/associations: Idiopathic or familial forms; lung diseases (like COPD or fibrosis); heart valve problems or heart failure; chronic blood clots; autoimmune diseases; HIV; certain medicines.
- Typical symptoms: Shortness of breath with activity, fatigue, leg swelling, chest pain or lightheadedness.
- Treatments: Treat the underlying cause when possible; medicines to widen blood vessels (oral, IV, or inhaled); oxygen therapy; blood thinners if indicated; manage sleep apnea; in some cases, lung or heart-lung transplant.
- Next steps and cautions: Prognosis varies by cause; requires specialist care. Avoid pregnancy in many cases and follow with a pulmonary hypertension or cardiology team. Seek urgent care if chest pain or severe shortness of breath occurs.

This information is general; individual risk and decisions depend on specific circumstances and should be discussed with a qualified professional.
